did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and Dual-Path Spatial Attention Network) integrated into an ensemble model for skin cancer classification. It also employed Explainable AI methods (LIME, Saliency Maps, SmoothGrad) to interpret the model's predictions on dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an ensemble deep learning model (combining ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and DPSAN) for skin cancer diagnosis using the ISIC dataset. It includes a proposed methodology, dataset description, results with specific performance metrics (e.g., 94.69% validation accuracy), and analysis of Explainable AI techniques, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCIKE), it reports original results.

title: Enhancing skin cancer diagnosis using ensemble based deep learning models with Explainable AI
journal: 2025 International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Knowledge Economy (ICCIKE)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer diagnosis by developing an ensemble deep learning model that integrates ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and the Dual-Path Spatial Attention Network (DPSAN). The models were trained and evaluated on the ISIC dataset, which contains 2,357 dermoscopic images covering nine distinct skin cancer classes. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the performance of individual architectures against the proposed ensemble method using validation accuracy and loss metrics. The ensemble model achieved the highest validation accuracy of 94.69% and the lowest loss of 0.148, outperforming all individual models. To enhance clinical trust and interpretability, the study employed Explainable AI techniques, including LIME, Saliency Maps, and SmoothGrad, to visualize the regions of interest influencing the model's predictions. The results demonstrate that combining these architectures significantly improves classification performance while providing transparent explanations for dermatologists.

all_task_types.target: Nine distinct skin cancer classes (Dermatofibroma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer using an ensemble of ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and DPSAN models. The output is a probability vector over nine classes via softmax activation, with the final prediction determined by the greatest probability in the fused output. Validation accuracy (94.69%) and loss (0.148) are used as metrics.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: Original dermoscopic images obtained from the International Skin Imaging Collaborative archive, containing a variety of benign and malignant oncological skin disorders.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and DPSAN models within the ensemble framework.
use.validation: Used for validation to evaluate model performance (e.g., achieving 94.69% validation accuracy for the ensemble model).
use.other: Used for generating explanations via Explainable AI methods (LIME, Saliency Maps, SmoothGrad) to interpret model predictions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on validation accuracy and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset.metric: Validation Accuracy, Validation Loss, Training Time (seconds), Epochs
ISIC Dataset.notes: The ensemble model outperformed all individual models with the highest validation accuracy (94.69%) and lowest loss (0.148). DPSAN had the highest accuracy among individual models (93.86%) but also the highest loss (0.8783). ResNet-50 offered a balanced trade-off with the lowest loss among individual models (0.2252). EN-B7 required the most computation time (4759.4s).

XAI Evaluation (on Ensemble Model).notes: LIME was noted as particularly effective for instance-level explanations and clinical validation, offering the most detailed and locally interpretable information. SmoothGrad reduced visual noise compared to traditional Saliency Maps.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and Dual-Path Spatial Attention Network (DPSAN)
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 initialized with pretrained weights from ImageNet; DPSAN uses an EfficientNetV2-S backbone pretrained on ImageNet
architecture_used.input_size: ResNet-50: 224x224x3; EfficientNet-B7: 180x180x3; DPSAN: Not explicitly specified (uses EfficientNetV2-S backbone)

ResNet-50.head: Global Average Pooling (GAP) -> Dense layer (512 ReLU units) -> Dropout (50%) -> Softmax classifier (9 classes)

EfficientNet-B7.backbone: EfficientNet-B7 using compound scaling of depth, breadth, and resolution
EfficientNet-B7.blocks: Multiple Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) blocks with Squeeze-and-Excitation mechanisms

DPSAN.blocks: Sequence of DPSAN blocks (Batch Normalization, Attention Module, Convolutional Layer)
DPSAN.attention_mechanism: Spatial attention using 7x7 convolution over concatenated AvgPool and MaxPool descriptors
DPSAN.head: Global Average Pooling (GAP) -> Dense layer (512 ReLU units) -> Dropout -> Softmax classifier (9 classes)

Ensemble.fusion_method: Weighted average of softmax output probability vectors: f_ensemble(x) = α·f_R(x) + β·f_E(x) + γ·f_D(x) where α+β+γ=1
Ensemble.final_prediction: Class with greatest probability in fused output

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (implied Cross-Entropy via softmax)

preprocessing_techniques: Input resizing to specific dimensions: ResNet-50 (224x224x3), EfficientNet-B7 (180x180x3)
Batch Normalization applied within network layers to stabilize and speed up training
Global Average Pooling (GAP) used to compress feature maps into single vectors before classification
conclusions: The proposed ensemble model, combining ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B7, and Dual-Path Spatial Attention Network (DPSAN), outperformed all individual architectures with a validation accuracy of 94.69% and the lowest validation loss of 0.148.
Among individual models, DPSAN achieved the highest validation accuracy (93.86%), while ResNet-50 offered a balanced trade-off with the lowest loss (0.2252) among single models.
EfficientNet-B7 required significantly more computation time (4759.4 seconds) compared to other models, despite having comparable accuracy (91.25%).

The ensemble approach effectively aggregated the strengths of base architectures (ResNet-50’s residual depth, EN-B7’s compound efficiency, and DPSAN’s spatial focus) to improve classification accuracy, resilience, and generalization.

Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically LIME, Saliency Maps, and SmoothGrad, were successfully employed to provide interpretable explanations for model predictions, enhancing clinical trust and transparency.

LIME was identified as particularly effective for instance-level explanations, while SmoothGrad improved upon traditional Saliency Maps by reducing visual noise and providing smoother, more robust heatmaps.
The ensemble model demonstrated robustness against image quality fluctuations and reduced misclassification rates, making it suitable for real-world diagnostic support systems.

The study highlights the critical need for explainability in medical AI to bridge the gap between high-performance black-box models and clinical adoption, ensuring physicians can validate model judgments.
important_remarks: The study relies on a relatively small dataset of only 2,357 dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive, which may limit the generalizability of the deep learning models to broader, more diverse clinical populations.
The paper does not report key training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, learning rate, or batch size, which hinders reproducibility and assessment of training stability.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the performance differences between the ensemble model and individual architectures.
The dataset contains nine classes with significant potential class imbalance (common in ISIC data), yet the paper does not report per-class metrics or discuss how class imbalance was addressed.

The EfficientNet-B7 model required disproportionately high computational resources (4,759.4 seconds) compared to its marginal accuracy gain over other models, raising concerns about deployment efficiency.
The ensemble fusion weights (alpha, beta, gamma) are defined theoretically but their specific values used in the experiment are not explicitly reported.
The work is published as a conference paper in a 2025 IEEE conference, which may have less rigorous peer review standards compared to top-tier medical AI journals.

Explainable AI (XAI) results are presented qualitatively and via limited quantitative metrics (Pixel IoU, Fidelity) without clinical validation by dermatologists to confirm the clinical relevance of the explanations.
